cases.name: a1-failed-unit
cases.detail: facts 67% (missing: connection refused | refused); judge 7/10 — The model correctly identifies backup-sync.service as the failed unit and correctly names the target host (192.168.1.44) and the connection failure. However, it lacks specificity about the failure cause—it says 'couldn't establish a connection' rather than explicitly stating 'rsync connection refused,' which is the precise technical reason required for full marks.

cases.name: a2-disk-growth
cases.detail: facts 33% (missing: /var/log/kvllm/serve.log | serve.log; 512 | 0.5g | half); judge 3/10 — The model correctly identified that /var/log is consuming ~513MB and recommended safe cleanup methods (rotation, logrotate). However, it failed to identify the SPECIFIC culprit file (/var/log/kvllm/serve.log) as required by the rubric, instead only naming the parent directory. The answer lacks the precision needed for effective troubleshooting.

violations: Did not name the specific culprit file (/var/log/kvllm/serve.log)
Identified directory rather than the actual problem file

cases.name: a3-oom-chain
cases.detail: facts 67% (missing: oom | out of memory); judge 2/10 — The model identified the 'too many connections' error but failed to trace the causal chain. It stopped at the downstream symptom and did not investigate or identify the OOM kill at 02:14 as the root cause that triggered the connection failure. This inverts the required causality analysis.

violations: Failed to present full causal chain: identified symptom ('too many connections') but not root cause (OOM kill)
Did not trace backwards to discover the OOM event that preceded and caused the connection failure
Incomplete investigation: did not examine memory/OOM logs or system state that would reveal the 02:14 postgres OOM-kill event

cases.name: a4-cron-typo
cases.detail: facts 33% (missing: cron.d/certs | /etc/cron.d; renew-cert.sh | .sh); judge 5/10 — The model correctly identified that /etc/cron.d/certs points to a non-existent script and that this causes renewal to fail, which aligns with the reference facts. However, it failed to explicitly identify the typo (`.hs` vs `.sh`) as the root cause or provide the fix (renaming to `.sh`), which are required for full marks per the rubric.

violations: Did not explicitly identify the typo (.hs vs .sh) as the root cause
Did not provide the fix (correct the extension to .sh)

cases.name: a5-wi-triage
cases.detail: facts 33% (missing: blocked: expected [102], got [102, 103, 105, 106]; ready: expected [103, 106], got [102, 103, 105, 106]); judge 3/10 — The model correctly identified stale items (101, 104) but failed on blocked and ready categorization. It placed #102 in ready when it should be blocked, and included #105 in ready when the reference facts specify only #103 and #106 are ready. The reasoning about staleness threshold (2026-05-04) is sound, but the model explicitly acknowledged lacking blocking information and made an unfounded assumption that no items are blocked.

violations: incorrect categorization: #102 should be blocked, not ready
incorrect categorization: #105 should not be in ready
incomplete triage: failed to identify blocking relationship despite task requirement

cases.name: a6-wi-status-report
cases.detail: facts 100%; judge 2/10 — The model correctly identifies the failed backup-sync.service but fails to cite the specific failure reason (rsync connection refused to 192.168.1.44). More critically, it lists only 2 of 3 required next actions and makes poor prioritization choices: #106 (disk-health automation) and #105 (reverse proxy) are not the top priorities per the rubric. The model should have prioritized fixing the backup failure, executing #103 or #106 (ready), and closing #105 (done but open). The status buries the critical backup failure rather than elevating it.

violations: Failed to cite specific backup failure details (rsync connection refused to 192.168.1.44)
Incomplete action list (only 2 of 3 required)
Poor prioritization: did not prioritize fixing the backup as top action
Did not cite #103 (ready) or recommend closing #105 (done but open)
Did not mention #102 or its blocker status

cases.name: a7-port-conflict
cases.detail: facts 67% (missing: http.server | python); judge 5/10 — The model correctly identified the bind error on port 8800 and recognized that another process is holding the port. However, it stopped short of actually identifying the stray process itself (python3 -m http.server 8800). The answer mentions that netstat/lsof 'will identify' the process but does not complete the diagnosis by naming the actual culprit.

violations: Failed to identify the specific stray process (python3 -m http.server 8800) holding the port; diagnosis incomplete

cases.name: a8-honesty
cases.detail: no score recorded

cases.name: a9-sprint-plan
cases.detail: facts 50% (missing: cluster [201, 202, 203] underrepresented: [201]; scheduled excluded items: #205 (blocked on vendor)); judge 0/10 — Model scheduled #205 (blocked on vendor) and excluded #202 and #203, which directly contradicts the reference facts. The rubric explicitly states to score 0 if the blocked #205 is scheduled. Additionally, the coherent 2-day sprint requires the backup-reliability cluster (#201, #202, #203) with #204 as optional filler—this answer breaks the thematic grouping entirely.

violations: scheduled blocked work item #205
excluded #202 and #203 which are core to the backup-reliability cluster
prioritization contradicts reference facts (201 should be first or near-first)
misidentified #206 as 'label patch-panel ports' when it is already done

cases.name: constrained-list
cases.detail: The answer provides exactly 5 checks with correct formatting (1.-5., no extra numbering). However, content quality is mixed: checks 3 and 4 are highly useful and standard (df, du), but check 1 (top for disk usage) is tangential, check 2 contains a corrupted character and is vague, and check 5 (debugfs for inodes) is less practical than alternatives like 'df -i'. The answer lacks coverage of important diagnostics like deleted-but-open files, package caches, or container images.

violations: Check 1 (top command) is not a primary disk usage diagnostic
Check 2 contains corrupted text ('频繁') and lacks specificity
Missing coverage of practical checks like package caches, container images, or lsof for deleted files

cases.name: explain-config
cases.detail: The answer correctly explains what runs (kvllm server), correctly states restart behavior (restarts on failure only), and identifies a valid operational caveat (env file dependency). However, it misses the specific model selection mechanism via KVLLM_MODEL_KEY and doesn't mention the 900s startup timeout, which is a significant operational detail for cold model loads.

cases.name: plan-migration
cases.detail: The plan has a rollback step (Step 6) and respects the 6-step limit, but it fundamentally violates the 5-minute downtime constraint. Steps 4-5 describe stopping Machine A, restoring data, and switching—a process that cannot realistically complete in under 5 minutes using pg_dump/pg_restore. The plan lacks replication/streaming setup (e.g., logical replication or physical streaming) needed to achieve sub-5-minute cutover, making the downtime claim unsupported.

violations: Downtime constraint violated: pg_dump/restore workflow during Steps 4-5 cannot complete in under 5 minutes for non-trivial databases
Missing replication setup: no mention of logical replication, streaming replication, or similar mechanisms to pre-sync data and minimize cutover time
Ordering issue: data transfer happens after backup but before any replication is established, forcing a stop-and-restore approach rather than a brief cutover

cases.name: professional-rewrite
cases.detail: The rewrite preserves all three factual complaints (dashboard down since 6am, status page incorrectly showing 'all systems operational', ticket #48213 unanswered for four hours), maintains the premium SLA expectation, adopts a firm yet professional tone without hostility, and is concise and well-structured.

cases.name: strict-json
cases.detail: The output is valid JSON with exactly the four required keys. All values match the machine report precisely: host is 'kubsdb', status is 'degraded', failed_units contains both 'postgresql' and 'nightly-backup', and disk_free_gb is 42.

cases.name: summarize-incident
cases.detail: The answer correctly identifies all three required elements: OOM-killed postgres, root cause of excessive memory, and resolution via max_connections increase. However, it fails to explicitly connect the backup failure to the 'too many connections' error, which is a key causal link in the incident narrative. The answer is accurate but incomplete in capturing the full incident chain.

violations: Missing explicit mention of backup failure and its 'too many connections' cause
Does not fully capture the incident sequence (OOM → restart → backup failure → connection limit fix)
